content.text: Even the most well-designed approved model registry cannot anticipate every legitimate business need. A research team may need to evaluate a newly released model before it has completed the standard review cycle; an urgent customer requirement may necessitate a capability that only an unapproved vendor provides; an acquisition may bring an existing system that uses non-approved models. The exception and waiver process provides a governed, time-limited path to use a model outside the approved registry, without undermining the registry's security and compliance value.
What it is

A model approval exception is a formal governance decision that permits use of a model not currently in the approved registry, under defined conditions, for a defined period, and with compensating controls that partially offset the risks of the missing review. The waiver process is the administrative workflow through which an exception is requested, assessed, decided, and recorded. Exceptions are not a bypass of governance—they are an alternative governance path with higher scrutiny and shorter tenure than a full registry approval.
Why it matters

Without a formal exception process, developers bypass the registry informally: they use unapproved models and simply do not tell governance. An exception process channels this natural pressure for flexibility into a documented, auditable path. Regulators accept exception-based frameworks provided exceptions are time-limited, documented, and subject to compensating controls. An exception process also generates useful signal: a high volume of exception requests for the same model indicates the registry review process should be expedited for that model.
Exception categories
PoC exceptions

Research and evaluation of a new model before it has completed the standard 4–6 week review cycle. PoC exceptions are time-limited (30 days maximum), restricted to non-production environments, and prohibit processing of personal data above the model's anticipated tier. They are the most common exception type and should have a lightweight approval path: CISO or delegate can approve without full steering committee review.
Urgent business exceptions

A customer requirement or regulatory deadline necessitates a capability only available from an unapproved model. Urgent exceptions require CISO and legal co-approval, must document the specific capability gap and why no approved model can meet it, and are time-limited to 90 days while the full review is expedited. They require more stringent compensating controls: data anonymisation before model calls, enhanced monitoring, and a named engineer accountable for the exception.
Inherited exceptions (M&A or legacy systems)

A system acquired through merger or already in production uses a model not in the registry. Inherited exceptions are time-limited to 180 days, during which the inherited model must either be added to the registry through full review or replaced. They require isolation: the inherited system must not be integrated with other enterprise data until the review is complete or a replacement is deployed.
Security exception for critical model patches

A special case of the exception process arises when a vendor releases a critical security patch in a new model version that has not yet gone through the standard 4–6 week review cycle. In this scenario, blocking the patched version until the review completes leaves agents running on a known-vulnerable model version, which is the worse security outcome. Define a security fast-track exception: the CISO may approve a version upgrade without full steering committee review within 24 hours, provided the new version is from an already-approved model family, the patch addresses a CVSS 7+ vulnerability, and the version change is minor (no breaking API changes). The full review is then completed within 30 days as a retrospective validation.
Tracking exception conversions and rejections

At each exception expiry, one of three outcomes occurs: the model completes the full registry review and is added to the registry (conversion); the team finds an alternative approved model and stops using the exception model (voluntary discontinuation); or the exception expires without a resolution and the model is blocked (enforcement). Track these outcomes quarterly. A high rejection rate (models failing the full review after being used under exception) indicates that exceptions are being approved too permissively. A high conversion rate indicates the exception process is functioning as an effective fast-track pathway. Target 60–70% conversion, 20–30% voluntary discontinuation, and under 10% enforcement.
Multi-team exception coordination

When multiple teams simultaneously request exceptions for the same model, avoid processing them independently. Consolidate into a single exception decision: approve or reject for all teams, with shared compensating controls. This reduces review workload, ensures consistency, and accelerates conversion to a full registry entry—since a multi-team use case creates stronger business justification for expediting the full review. Assign a lead team as the exception owner responsible for coordinating the full review and communicating the outcome to all other teams using the exception.
Exception documentation standards for regulatory evidence

Regulators conducting AI governance audits will ask for evidence that your exception process is rigorous. Maintain exception records in a format that supports evidence production: each exception record must include the original request, the risk classification performed, the approval decision with rationale, the compensating controls actually implemented (not just stated), and the outcome at expiry. Store these records for a minimum of 3 years after the exception expires. For exceptions involving personal data (urgent exceptions above T2 tier), also maintain a record in your Article 30 Records of Processing Activities for GDPR compliance.
Linking exception tracking to the CI/CD pipeline

Exception enforcement is only effective if the CI/CD pipeline knows which models are currently under exception and enforces the expiry automatically. When an exception is approved, write the exception record to the registry API with status='exception' and expiry_date. The CI/CD model validation step treats exception status identically to approved status during the exception period. On expiry_date, the registry API automatically transitions the status to expired, and the next CI/CD run blocks deployment of any agent code referencing that model. This automated enforcement prevents exceptions from silently persisting past their expiry dates without developer action.
Exception process calibration and annual review

The exception process itself requires periodic calibration. Annually review the exception policy: are the category definitions still appropriate? Are maximum durations set correctly? Do the approval authorities have the right level of seniority given current risk levels? Survey teams that have used the exception process to identify friction points: a process that takes 15 business days to approve a PoC exception will be bypassed through informal channels. The goal is a process that is fast enough to be used willingly and rigorous enough to provide genuine governance value. Adjust approval thresholds, delegate additional approval authority, or reduce compensating control requirements for lower-risk exception categories based on the annual calibration findings.
Exception process for open-source models

Open-source model exceptions present a different risk profile from commercial vendor exceptions. An open-source model (e.g. Llama 3, Mistral) has no vendor DPA to sign—the licensing terms govern the relationship. The exception risk assessment for open-source models focuses on different dimensions: the model's safety alignment (is it fine-tuned for safety, or is it a base model?), the hosting environment's security controls (who controls the inference infrastructure?), and the licence terms (does the licence restrict commercial use or require open-sourcing of derivatives?). Build a dedicated open-source exception sub-process that addresses these questions instead of the standard vendor DPA review.
Communicating exception decisions to affected teams

When an exception is rejected, the affected team needs to understand why and what alternative paths exist. A rejection notification should include: the specific risk that was not adequately mitigated, the compensating control that would have been needed to approve, the approved model that can meet the use case at a lower risk level, and the timeline for the rejected model to complete full review if applicable. Rejection notifications that only cite 'does not meet governance standards' without actionable guidance create frustration and increase informal bypass. Make rejection communication an accountable step in the exception workflow, assigned to the approver with a 2-business-day deadline.
Exception metrics in the AI governance scorecard

Include exception metrics in your AI governance scorecard: active exception count, exception conversion rate (exceptions that became full approvals), average time-to-decision, and exception volume trend quarter-over-quarter. Present these to the AI steering committee alongside registry coverage and model review cycle time. Together these metrics show whether the model sourcing policy is functioning as an effective governance framework or whether it is generating friction that drives shadow adoption. A governance framework that is consistently bypassed is not a governance framework—it is documentation.
Sunset clause for the exception framework itself

As the approved model registry matures and model review timelines improve, the need for exceptions should decrease. Build a sunset clause into the exception policy: if average model review time falls below 10 business days and registry coverage exceeds 95% of models in active use, the exception process is replaced with a streamlined 5-business-day fast-track review rather than an exception. This creates an incentive for the governance team to invest in review efficiency rather than treating the exception process as a permanent feature of the governance landscape.
Architecture diagram

attrs.alt: Model approval exception workflow: request steps and possible outcomes

content.text: When to use this
When a team needs to evaluate a model not yet in the registry before the standard review cycle completes.
When a business deadline requires a capability that only an unapproved model provides.
When an acquired system or legacy deployment uses a non-registered model.
When a vendor releases a critical security patch in a new model version that has not yet been approved.
How to implement
Define exception categories, approval authorities, and maximum durations in the AI governance policy.
Build an exception request form: model ID, use case, data classification, duration, compensating controls, and business justification.
Route PoC exceptions to CISO for single-approver decision; route urgent and inherited exceptions to the steering committee.
Track all active exceptions in the registry with status (active/expired/converted_to_full_approval).
Set automated expiry alerts at 30 days and 7 days before exception end date.
Publish a quarterly exception report to the AI steering committee showing exception volume, categories, and conversion-to-registry rates.
Implementation example

# Exception request template
exception_request:
  id: EXC-2025-042
  requested_by: "data-science-team@company.com"
  date: "2025-07-10"
  model_id: "new-vendor-reasoning-v2"
  vendor: "NewVendorAI"
  category: PoC  # PoC | Urgent | Inherited

  business_justification: >
    Evaluating new reasoning capabilities for complex contract analysis workflow.
    No approved model achieves >85% accuracy on our benchmark; this model achieves 94%.
    Evaluation requires 30 days of testing on synthetic data.

  data_classification: internal  # no PII in PoC phase
  environment: non-production  # sandbox only
  duration_days: 30
  expiry_date: "2025-08-09"

  compensating_controls:
    - "All prompts use synthetic data only; no real customer data"
    - "Output logs reviewed daily by named engineer"
    - "Rate limit set to 100 requests/day to limit blast radius"
    - "Full registry review initiated in parallel; target completion 2025-08-01"

  approver: "ciso@company.com"
  decision: approved
  decision_date: "2025-07-11"
  conditions:
    - "PoC restricted to sandbox environment"
    - "Full review must complete before any production use"
    - "Exception auto-expires 2025-08-09"
Real-world example

A legal technology company received an urgent request from a major client to demonstrate contract-risk analysis capabilities within 45 days. The only model achieving the required accuracy was from a vendor not yet in the approved registry. The AI governance team approved an urgent exception: 90 days of limited production use (only for that client's anonymised contracts, with enhanced output monitoring), while expediting the full registry review. The review completed in 60 days; the model was added to the registry at T4 tier before the exception expired, converting the temporary arrangement into a permanent approved capability.
Common mistakes
No expiry enforcement:
exceptions without automated expiry become permanent—build expiry into the workflow, not just the document.
Approving exceptions without compensating controls:
an exception with no additional controls provides no more governance than ignoring the registry.
Not tracking exception-to-registry conversion rate:
if fewer than 50% of exceptions convert to full approvals, the registry review process may be too slow or the registry criteria too restrictive.
Exception process replacing the standard review for convenience:
high exception volume from the same team for the same vendor is a sign that the standard review should be expedited, not that exceptions should be granted routinely.
Cost accounting for exception usage

Models used under exception are not in the approved registry and therefore lack cost_per_Mtok entries. This creates a gap in FinOps attribution. Require that all exception requests include a cost estimate: the requestor must query the vendor's public pricing and record the estimated cost per million tokens in the exception record. The FinOps team then creates a temporary registry entry with exception status that maps to this cost parameter, enabling cost attribution to continue uninterrupted. At exception conversion or expiry, the temporary entry is either upgraded to a full registry entry or removed, with all historical spend attributed to the exception period.
Best practices
Set a policy cap: no team may hold more than two active exceptions simultaneously.
Require that a full registry review is initiated in parallel with any exception beyond PoC category.
Track time-to-approval for exception requests as a governance KPI; target under 5 business days for PoC, 10 for urgent.
Publish exception decisions (redacted where commercially sensitive) to demonstrate transparency to auditors.
Performance and scalability

Exception volume will grow as the number of teams and use cases expands. Prevent exception-process bottlenecks by empowering delegated approvers: the CISO may authorise a named deputy to approve PoC exceptions independently, reducing the time for the most common exception type. Monthly exception volume reporting to the steering committee allows the governance team to detect and address systematic issues—such as a category of models that repeatedly appears in exception requests but has not been prioritised for registry review.
Exception volume as a governance health metric

A low exception volume (less than 5% of model adoption events) indicates the registry is comprehensive and review timelines are acceptable. A high exception volume (above 15%) indicates the registry is not keeping pace with market model releases, review timelines are too slow, or the registry criteria are too restrictive. Review exception volume quarterly and use it to calibrate the registry programme: add more review capacity, fast-track high-demand vendor reviews, or adjust the tier criteria if specific use cases are consistently driving exceptions.
Tooling and ecosystem
Tool
Role
Jira / ServiceNow workflow
Exception request submission, routing, and approval tracking
Registry API
Auto-block models on registry with status=denied; enforce exception expiry
Email/Slack automation
Expiry alerts at 30d and 7d before exception end date
Governance dashboard
Track exception volume, conversion rate, and time-to-decision KPIs
Comparison with alternatives
No exception process (strict registry only):
enforces governance but creates business friction that drives shadow AI adoption; not practical for innovative teams.
Informal exceptions (verbal approval):
fast but unauditable; fails regulatory scrutiny and creates governance gaps.
Open registry (any model permitted until rejected):
eliminates exception volume but inverts the security posture from allow-list to block-list; not appropriate for regulated industries.
Security and reliability

Exception approvals are security decisions. Each approved exception represents an accepted risk that is recorded and time-limited. Maintain an exception risk register that documents the risk level (based on model tier and data classification), the compensating controls, and the residual risk accepted by the approver. This register is evidence for auditors that your governance framework treats exceptions as risk management decisions, not administrative formalities.
Key takeaways
Design the exception process as a higher-scrutiny governance path, not a bypass—every exception must document business justification, duration, and compensating controls.
Differentiate exception categories (PoC, urgent, inherited) with different approval authorities and maximum durations.
Automate exception expiry enforcement; a manually tracked exception is an exception that will be forgotten and extended indefinitely.
Use exception volume as a governance health metric; persistent high volume signals a need to improve the standard review process.
Require that a full registry review runs in parallel with any exception beyond the PoC category.
